does: This paper presents RLearner-LLM with Hybrid-DPO, a method that addresses a systematic failure mode in Direct Preference Optimization for knowledge-intensive tasks. The core contribution is an automated preference signal pipeline that combines a DeBERTa-v3 NLI (Natural Language Inference) classifier with a verifier LLM score, eliminating reliance on human annotators while balancing logical correctness against fluency. Evaluated on knowledge-intensive domains including Biology, Medicine, and Law across multiple base models (LLaMA-2-13B, Qwen3-8B, Gemma 4 E4B-it), the method achieves up to 6x improvement in NLI entailment scores over standard supervised fine-tuning, with NLI gains observed in 11 of 15 experimental configurations. The approach successfully scales to compact models with reduced parameters while maintaining inference efficiency improvements across all tested domains.
connects: This work extends recent advances in efficient RLHF alternatives, specifically building on Direct Preference Optimization's lightweight alternative to PPO-based methods. It responds directly to emerging research identifying systematic biases in preference signals, particularly the verbosity bias that prioritizes fluent-sounding responses over factual grounding. The paper synthesizes established techniques—NLI scoring from entailment models and LLM-based verification—within a hybrid preference framework, drawing on prior work demonstrating that single-signal optimization creates alignment tradeoffs. It engages with the broader conversation around knowledge-grounding in language models and the challenge of evaluating logical correctness in generation, building on foundation work in textual entailment and automated verification approaches.
enables: This approach opens pathways for automated, human-annotation-free preference optimization across knowledge-intensive domains, potentially accelerating adaptation of language models to specialized fields without expensive human labor. The hybrid signal methodology could be generalized beyond NLI to incorporate domain-specific verification signals, enabling fine-grained alignment control for different downstream applications. The demonstrated scaling properties on compact models suggest practical deployment scenarios for knowledge-grounded systems with computational constraints. Future work could extend this to other reasoning tasks, investigate compositional verification for complex logical chains, or develop adaptive weighting schemes that dynamically balance competing optimization signals. The framework also positions opportunities for developing lightweight, domain-specialized verifiers that could enable similar improvements for other factuality-critical generation tasks.
